flashcards.back: Binding is the process of associating a function call with its actual function implementation. It determines which function gets executed when a call is made.
Early binding (static binding) occurs at compile time. The compiler determines which function to call based on the object's type known at compile time. Function overloading uses early binding.
Late binding (dynamic binding) occurs at runtime. The actual function to execute is determined during program execution based on the object's actual type. Virtual functions enable late binding.

flashcards.front: What enables late binding in C++?
flashcards.back: Virtual functions enable late binding. When a function is declared as virtual, the compiler creates a VTABLE for the class and uses a VPTR to resolve function calls at runtime.
VTABLE (Virtual Table) is a lookup table created by the compiler for each class that has virtual functions. It contains pointers to the virtual function implementations for that class.
VPTR (Virtual Table Pointer) is a hidden pointer inserted by the compiler in each object. It points to the VTABLE of the class. Used for runtime polymorphism.
Runtime polymorphism is achieved through late binding using virtual functions. It allows the same function call to behave differently based on the actual object type at runtime.

flashcards.front: What is a pure virtual function?
flashcards.back: A pure virtual function is declared using '= 0' and has no implementation. It makes a class abstract. Derived classes must override it.
An abstract class contains at least one pure virtual function. It cannot be instantiated but can be used as a base class for derived classes.
Virtual destructors ensure proper cleanup when a derived class object is deleted through a base class pointer. Without virtual destructor, only base destructor is called causing memory leak.
Function overloading is compile-time polymorphism where multiple functions have the same name but different parameters. Uses early binding.
Function overriding occurs when a derived class provides a new implementation for a virtual function from base class. Enables late binding and runtime polymorphism.
RTTI (Runtime Type Information) is a mechanism in C++ that provides type information about objects at runtime. Used with dynamic_cast and typeid.

flashcards.front: Performance difference between early and late binding?
flashcards.back: Early binding is faster as function call resolution happens at compile time. Late binding has runtime overhead due to VTABLE lookup but offers flexibility.
Object slicing occurs when a derived class object is assigned to a base class object by value. It removes the derived part, losing polymorphism. Use pointers/references to avoid slicing.
